content.body: Einstein's Special Theory of Relativity (1905) revolutionised our understanding of space and time. It applies to objects moving at speeds close to the speed of light.

Postulates:
1. Laws of physics are same in all inertial frames
2. Speed of light c = 3×10⁸ m/s is same for all observers

Consequences:
Time dilation: t = t₀/√(1-v²/c²) = γt₀
Length contraction: L = L₀√(1-v²/c²) = L₀/γ
Relativistic mass: m = γm₀
Mass-energy: E = mc² = γm₀c²
γ = Lorentz factor = 1/√(1-v²/c²)

content.title: Relativistic Effects
content.body:
Spacecraft at v=0.6c:
γ = 1/√(1-0.36) = 1/√0.64 = 1/0.8 = 1.25
Time dilation: 1 hour on spacecraft = 1.25 hours to observer
Length contraction: 100m spacecraft appears 80m long

Muon paradox: cosmic ray muons travel 10km to Earth in their proper lifetime of ~2.2μs (impossible classically) — explained by time dilation

content.title: Mass-Energy Equivalence
content.body:
E=mc² means mass IS energy
Nuclear bomb: tiny mass converted to enormous energy
1g mass → E=0.001×(3×10⁸)²=9×10¹³ J (equivalent to 21.5 kilotons TNT)
Sun loses 4 million tonnes per second by E=mc²

questions.question: Special relativity applies to
options: objects at speeds comparable to speed of light
questions.answer: objects at speeds comparable to speed of light
questions.explanation: Special relativity: significant effects only at speeds approaching c (~3×10⁸ m/s)

questions.question: Speed of light in vacuum
options: is constant c=3×10⁸m/s for all observers
questions.answer: is constant c=3×10⁸m/s for all observers
questions.explanation: 2nd postulate of special relativity: c is invariant — same in all inertial frames regardless of motion

questions.question: Time dilation means
questions.explanation: Moving clock ticks more slowly: t=γt₀. Observer sees dilated (longer) time intervals

questions.question: Lorentz factor γ at v=0.8c = 1/√(1-0.64) = 1/√0.36 =
Length contraction: at v=0.8c (γ=1.67), 100m rocket appears to stationary observer to be
questions.explanation: L=L₀/γ=100/1.67≈60m (length contracts along direction of motion)

questions.question: E=mc²: 1kg of matter fully converted to energy gives
questions.explanation: E=1×(3×10⁸)²=9×10¹⁶J — enormously more than chemical energy from 1kg

questions.question: At what speed does relativistic time dilation become significant?
questions.explanation: Relativistic effects small at low speeds (γ≈1), become significant when v/c > 0.1

questions.question: Muon paradox demonstrates
options: time dilation — fast-moving muons survive to Earth due to dilated proper lifetime
questions.answer: time dilation — fast-moving muons survive to Earth due to dilated proper lifetime
questions.explanation: Muons from cosmic rays travel ~10km at 0.99c. Classical: should decay in ~0.66km. Survive because their lifetime is dilated

questions.question: An object's mass increases with speed because
options: relativistic mass m=γm₀ increases with velocity
questions.answer: relativistic mass m=γm₀ increases with velocity
questions.explanation: Relativistic momentum requires increased effective mass at high speed. At c, would need infinite energy

questions.question: Special relativity is 'special' because
options: it applies only to special materials
it is restricted to non-accelerating inertial frames (general relativity covers acceleration)
questions.answer: it is restricted to non-accelerating inertial frames (general relativity covers acceleration)
questions.explanation: Special = restricted to inertial (non-accelerating) frames. General relativity (1915) extends to accelerating frames and gravity

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body:
General Relativity (Einstein, 1915):
Gravity is curvature of spacetime caused by mass/energy
Massive objects warp spacetime → other objects follow curved paths

Equivalence Principle:
Gravitational acceleration = inertial acceleration
(cannot distinguish gravity from acceleration)

Consequences:
Gravitational time dilation: clocks run slower in stronger gravity
t₀ = t√(1-2GM/rc²)
Gravitational redshift: photons lose energy climbing out of gravity well
Light bending: gravity deflects light paths

content.title: Black Holes
content.body:
Schwarzschild radius: rs = 2GM/c²
Sun: rs = 3km (compressed to 3km sphere)
Earth: rs = 9mm

At Schwarzschild radius: escape velocity = c → nothing escapes
Event horizon: boundary of no return
Singularity: point of infinite density at centre
Hawking radiation: quantum effect causes black holes to slowly evaporate

content.title: Gravitational Waves
content.body:
Predicted by GR (1916): ripples in spacetime from accelerating masses
Detected: LIGO 2015 (binary black hole merger)
Strain h = ΔL/L ≈ 10⁻²¹ (incredibly tiny)
Sources: merging black holes, neutron stars, supernovae
GW170817: first multi-messenger event (GW + gamma rays from neutron star merger)

questions.question: General relativity describes gravity as
questions.explanation: Einstein: gravity is not a force but geometry. Mass curves spacetime; objects follow geodesics (curved paths)

questions.question: Equivalence principle states
options: gravitational acceleration is indistinguishable from inertial acceleration
questions.answer: gravitational acceleration is indistinguishable from inertial acceleration
questions.explanation: In a closed box: free fall in gravity feels same as weightlessness; constant acceleration feels same as gravity

questions.question: Gravitational time dilation means
options: clocks run slower in stronger gravitational fields
time is relative to mass only
questions.answer: clocks run slower in stronger gravitational fields
questions.explanation: Strong gravity: spacetime more curved → time runs slower. GPS satellites must correct for this (they're in weaker gravity → tick faster)

questions.question: Schwarzschild radius of black hole is
options: event horizon radius where escape velocity = c
questions.answer: event horizon radius where escape velocity = c
questions.explanation: rs=2GM/c²: if object compressed to this radius, not even light escapes → black hole

questions.question: Gravitational waves were first directly detected by
questions.explanation: LIGO (Laser Interferometer Gravitational-wave Observatory) detected GW150914 from two merging ~30M☉ black holes

questions.question: Light bending around massive objects (gravitational lensing) was first confirmed
questions.explanation: Eddington's 1919 expedition: stars near Sun (visible during eclipse) appeared displaced by 1.75 arcseconds — matched GR prediction

questions.question: Gravitational redshift occurs when
options: photons lose energy climbing out of gravitational potential well
questions.answer: photons lose energy climbing out of gravitational potential well
questions.explanation: Photon escaping gravity: E=hf decreases (loses energy), f decreases → longer wavelength = redshift

questions.question: GPS satellites require relativistic corrections because
options: they move fast (special relativity slows clock)
they are in weaker gravity (general relativity speeds clock)
questions.explanation: Two competing effects: GPS satellite speed slows clock by 7μs/day (SR); weaker gravity speeds clock by 45μs/day (GR). Net: +38μs/day without correction → km error

questions.question: Hawking radiation predicts black holes
options: slowly evaporate due to quantum particle-antiparticle pairs near event horizon
questions.answer: slowly evaporate due to quantum particle-antiparticle pairs near event horizon
questions.explanation: Hawking (1974): virtual pairs at horizon — one falls in, other escapes as real radiation. Black hole loses mass and eventually evaporates

questions.question: The singularity at centre of black hole is a problem because
options: general relativity predicts infinite density/curvature where equations break down
questions.answer: general relativity predicts infinite density/curvature where equations break down
questions.explanation: Singularity: mathematical breakdown of GR. Quantum gravity theory needed to describe Planck-scale physics at singularity

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: Einstein's General Theory of Relativity (1915) extends special relativity to include gravity. Gravity is not a force but a curvature of spacetime caused by mass-energy.

Key predictions of GR:
1. Gravitational time dilation: clocks run slower in strong gravity
   Δt_g = Δt₀/√(1 - 2GM/rc²)
2. Gravitational lensing: light bends around massive objects
3. Gravitational waves: ripples in spacetime from accelerating masses
4. Perihelion precession of Mercury (confirmed 1915)
5. Black holes: regions where escape velocity > c

content.title: Experimental Confirmations
content.body:
1919: Eddington expedition — starlight bent by Sun (confirms GR)
1959: Pound-Rebka experiment — gravitational redshift confirmed
2015: LIGO detected gravitational waves from merging black holes
GPS satellites: must correct for both special and general relativistic time dilation to work accurately

Gravitational redshift: photon climbing out of gravity well loses energy → frequency decreases

content.title: Black Holes
content.body:
Schwarzschild radius: r_s = 2GM/c²
For Earth: r_s = 9mm (if compressed to this size)
For Sun: r_s = 3km

Event horizon: point of no return
Singularity: r=0, density infinite
Hawking radiation: quantum effect — BH slowly evaporates

questions.question: In general relativity, gravity is
options: curvature of spacetime caused by mass-energy
questions.answer: curvature of spacetime caused by mass-energy
questions.explanation: GR: massive objects curve the fabric of spacetime; other objects follow curved paths (geodesics) — this IS gravity

questions.question: Gravitational time dilation means
options: clocks near massive objects run MORE slowly
questions.answer: clocks near massive objects run MORE slowly
questions.explanation: Stronger gravity → greater spacetime curvature → slower time flow. Confirmed by atomic clocks at different altitudes

questions.question: GPS satellites need GR corrections because
options: without corrections, clocks drift (SR slows, GR speeds up) making GPS inaccurate by km/day
questions.answer: without corrections, clocks drift (SR slows, GR speeds up) making GPS inaccurate by km/day
questions.explanation: GPS: SR causes ~7μs/day slow-down; GR causes ~45μs/day speed-up. Net +38μs/day must be corrected

questions.question: Gravitational lensing is when
options: massive objects bend light paths, acting as cosmic lenses
questions.answer: massive objects bend light paths, acting as cosmic lenses
questions.explanation: GR predicts light follows curved spacetime. Confirmed 1919 (solar eclipse) — starlight deflected by Sun

questions.question: LIGO detected in 2015
options: gravitational waves from merging black holes
questions.answer: gravitational waves from merging black holes
questions.explanation: LIGO (Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory) detected spacetime ripples from two black holes merging 1.3 billion light-years away

questions.question: Schwarzschild radius defines
options: event horizon — radius within which escape velocity exceeds c
questions.answer: event horizon — radius within which escape velocity exceeds c
questions.explanation: r_s = 2GM/c²: if mass compressed within this radius, escape velocity = c → black hole forms

questions.question: Gravitational redshift: photon escaping strong gravity
questions.explanation: Photon climbing gravity well does work against gravity → loses energy → frequency decreases (redshift)

questions.question: Event horizon of black hole is
options: point of no return — nothing (not even light) can escape from within
questions.answer: point of no return — nothing (not even light) can escape from within
questions.explanation: Inside event horizon: escape velocity > c. All paths lead inward to singularity

questions.question: Hawking radiation predicts black holes
options: slowly evaporate due to quantum particle-antiparticle creation near event horizon
questions.answer: slowly evaporate due to quantum particle-antiparticle creation near event horizon
questions.explanation: Quantum vacuum fluctuations near event horizon: one particle escapes, one falls in → net energy loss → BH evaporates

questions.question: GR successfully explains Mercury's orbital precession (43 arcsec/century) which Newtonian gravity could not because
options: spacetime curvature near Sun adds extra orbital precession beyond Newtonian prediction
questions.answer: spacetime curvature near Sun adds extra orbital precession beyond Newtonian prediction
questions.explanation: Mercury's perihelion advances 43 arcsec/century more than Newton predicts — exact GR value confirmed Einstein's theory

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: Cosmology applies physics to study the universe as a whole — its origin, structure, and fate. Modern cosmology rests on both special and general relativity.

Hubble's Law:
v = H₀d
v = recession velocity (km/s)
d = distance (Mpc)
H₀ ≈ 70 km/s/Mpc (Hubble constant)

Age of universe ≈ 1/H₀ ≈ 13.8 billion years

Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB):
Blackbody radiation, T=2.725K
Relic from 380,000 years after Big Bang

content.title: Evidence for Big Bang
content.body:
1. Hubble's Law: all galaxies receding, farther→faster
2. CMB: uniform 2.725K background radiation
3. Nucleosynthesis: predicted H:He ratio (75:25) matches observations
4. Galaxy evolution: distant galaxies look younger

Dark matter: ~27% of universe — detected gravitationally, not yet directly observed
Dark energy: ~68% — causes accelerating expansion

content.title: Fate of the Universe
content.body:
Current observation: expansion accelerating (dark energy)
Big Freeze: expansion continues, stars die, heat death
Big Rip: dark energy overcomes all forces, tears everything apart
Big Crunch (unlikely): gravity overcomes dark energy, collapse

questions.question: Hubble's Law v=H₀d states
options: all galaxies move at same speed
galaxy recession velocity is proportional to its distance
questions.answer: galaxy recession velocity is proportional to its distance
questions.explanation: Hubble 1929: v∝d. Evidence universe is expanding — further galaxies recede faster

questions.question: Cosmic Microwave Background radiation is evidence for Big Bang because
options: it is uniform ~2.725K blackbody radiation — relic of hot early universe that has since cooled
questions.answer: it is uniform ~2.725K blackbody radiation — relic of hot early universe that has since cooled
questions.explanation: CMB: heat radiation from ~380,000 years after BB, cooled from 3000K to 2.725K as universe expanded 1000×

questions.question: The universe is approximately
questions.explanation: Age ≈ 1/H₀ ≈ 13.8 billion years (confirmed by CMB analysis and stellar ages)

questions.question: Dark matter is detected because
options: its gravitational effects (galaxy rotation curves, lensing) exceed visible matter
questions.answer: its gravitational effects (galaxy rotation curves, lensing) exceed visible matter
questions.explanation: Stars orbit galactic centres too fast for visible mass alone — unseen mass (dark matter) must provide extra gravity

questions.question: Redshift of distant galaxies is caused by
options: stretching of light waves as space expands (cosmological redshift)
questions.answer: stretching of light waves as space expands (cosmological redshift)
questions.explanation: Cosmological redshift: as universe expands, photon wavelengths stretch proportionally — more distant → greater redshift

questions.question: If H₀=70 km/s/Mpc and galaxy is 100 Mpc away, recession velocity=
Nucleosynthesis evidence for Big Bang predicts
options: 75% hydrogen, 25% helium by mass — matching observations
questions.answer: 75% hydrogen, 25% helium by mass — matching observations
questions.explanation: Big Bang nucleosynthesis: first 3 minutes produced H, He, Li in calculated ratios matching what we observe today

questions.question: Accelerating expansion was discovered in
questions.explanation: 1998: Perlmutter, Schmidt, Riess found distant supernovae fainter than expected — universe expanding FASTER than expected. Nobel Prize 2011

questions.question: Dark energy comprises approximately what fraction of the universe?
questions.explanation: Universe composition: ~68% dark energy, ~27% dark matter, ~5% ordinary (baryonic) matter

questions.question: The observable universe is limited because
options: we can only see nearby stars
light from regions more than ~46 billion light-years away has not had time to reach us
questions.answer: light from regions more than ~46 billion light-years away has not had time to reach us
questions.explanation: Observable universe: sphere of radius ~46 billion ly (expansion stretches this beyond 13.8 Gly). Beyond: light still en route

questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Relativity. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Relativity concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of physics.
Core Theory

This section of Relativity builds on foundational knowledge. In physics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Relativity. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Relativity. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Relativity include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Relativity. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.

options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.
